coredata.dc:title: One-pot green synthesis of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 hybrid nanoparticles from kelp for multi-modal imaging in vivo
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Wenping
Zeng, Ganmin
Yan, Jun
Liu, Xiaoli
Jiang, Xiaohong
Yang, Jiazhi
Liu, Jian
Sun, Dongping
coredata.dc:description: Multi-modal imaging technologies are playing an increasingly important role in biomedical research. However, there remains a demanding challenge to develop biocompatible contrast agents via a simple, green synthetic route for multi-modal imaging. Here we report the synthesis and applications of a new contrast agent for triple-modal imaging, that is, iodine-containing N-doping carbon nano-dots hybridized with Fe3O4 nanoparticles (I@CNDs-Fe3O4). We develop a one-pot, environment friendly hydrothermal method to synthesize the hybrid nanoparticles, primarily using kelp as the bioresource. I@CNDs-Fe3O4 nanoparticles have been demonstrated to exhibit excellent multi-modal imaging capabilities, including wavelength-tunable fluorescent imaging, X-ray attenuation for CT imaging enhancement, and T2-Weighted MR imaging. Importantly, the formulation of the hybrid nanoparticle provides an optimal solution to address the disequilibrium of osmotic pressure caused by the conventional CT imaging contrast agents of iodine compounds. The I@CNDs-Fe3O4 nanoparticles promise important applications in multi-modal imaging technologies in vivo as a versatile and biocompatible contrast agent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bioresource
Hybrid nanoparticles
Multi-modal imaging
CT
Osmotic pressure
Green synthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The in vitro and in vivo fluorescence imaging
Evaluation of cytotoxicity and fluorescent performance of I@CND-Fe3O4
The in vivo fluorescence imaging of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 on the zebra fish model

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51873087 and 51572124), Natural Science Foundation of China Jiangsu Province (grant number BE2018051), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (grant number 30920130121001), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (grant number 2018 M632307), and a Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD, China).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and morphological characterization. (a) Schematic illustration of one-pot green synthesis of I@CNDs-Fe3O4; (b-c) TEM images with different magnifications; (d) Particle size distribution histograms of I@CNDs-Fe3O4; (e) XRD pattern of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 and I@CNDs; (f) SEM image of I@CNDs-Fe3O4; (g) Size distribution of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 at day 1 and day 30. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) UV–vis absorption of Fe3O4, I@CNDs, and I@CNDs-Fe3O4 suspended in DI water; (b) Normalized FL emission spectra at excitation wavelengths from 320 to 520 nm; (c) The FL lifetime measurement and theoretically exponential fitting (τ1 = 1.05 ns, τ2 = 4.53 ns); (d) Photostability comparison of FITC (100 μg mL−1), and the I@CNDs-Fe3O4 (100 μg mL−1); e) Fluorescent images of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 deposited on glass coverslip after UV exposure (365 nm, power 250 W) for the specified duration. Scale bar: 25 μm); (f) Magnetic hysteresis loops of the I@CNDs-Fe3O4 at room temperature.
XPS spectral analysis. (a) XPS spectra of the I-CNDs and I@CNDs-Fe3O4; (b-f) High-resolution XPS spectral analysis of the I@CNDs-Fe3O4, including C1s, N1s, O1s, I3d, and Fe2p.

Osmotic pressure measurements. (a) The osmotic pressures of the KI, Iohexol and I@CNDs-Fe3O4 in DI water with the specified concentrations; (b) Zoomed-in view of the osmotic pressures of Iohexol and I@CNDs-Fe3O4 for comparison. The osmotic pressure of normal plasma (275–299 mOsm) as the reference (shown by the red dashed line). The orange and red columns represent two most widely used concentrations (300 and 350 mg mL−1) in clinical administration of iodinated compounds as CT imaging contrast agents. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Biocompatibility evaluations of I@CNDs-Fe3O4. (a-b) Cell viability assays for HUVEC and 4 T1 cells. Incubation time: 1 d and 2 d. Error bar: standard deviation (n = 5); (c-d) Fluorescent images of HUVEC and 4 T1 cells treated with I@CNDs-Fe3O4 under different excited wavelength (405 nm, 488 nm, and 522 nm). Objective (20×).

The in vivo FL images of I@CND-Fe3O4 on zebra fishes. (a) FL imaging of I@CND-Fe3O4 on zebra fish embryos; (b-g) The dynamic changes of I@CND-Fe3O4 on zebra fish larvae by FL imaging. Different time points of agent administration, stopping, and imaging are specified in the illustrative drawing. (excitation wavelengths: 405, 488, 522 nm; emission wavelength ranges: 420–500, 500–600, 622–760 nm).

CT imaging of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 in the zebra fish. (a) Schematic drawing of the longitudinal or transverse planes for fish sectioning; (b) The in vitro CT images of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 and Iohexol in agarose gel prosthesis; (c) Quantitative analysis of the performance (CT values, HU) of iohexol and I@CNDs-Fe3O4 in CT imaging contrast; (d) The in vivo CT images of zebra fish with or without the treatment of I@CNDs-Fe3O4. Red circles as a visual guide for comparison of the identical parts in fish sections. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

MR imaging of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 in the zebra fish. (a) The in vitro T2-weighted MR images of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 in agarose gel prosthesis. The white dashed triangle as a visual guide for the cross section of the gel prosthesis mixed with I@CNDs-Fe3O4 (400 μM); (b) Relaxation rate r2 (1/T2) versus various normalized molar concentrations of Fe element in I@CNDs-Fe3O4; (c) The in vivo T2-weighted MR images of zebra fish with or without the treatment of I@CNDs-Fe3O4. Red circles as a visual guide for comparison of the identical parts in fish sections. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: One-pot green synthesis of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 hybrid nanoparticles from kelp for multi-modal imaging in vivo

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Pharmacology and Safety Evaluation of Chinese Materia Medica, Department of Pharmacy, Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine, 138 Xianlin Avenue, Qixia District, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Pharmacology and Safety Evaluation of Chinese Materia Medica

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Pharmacology and Safety Evaluation of Chinese Materia Medica, Department of Pharmacy, Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine, 138 Xianlin Avenue, Qixia District, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210023, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multi-modal imaging technologies are playing an increasingly important role in biomedical research. However, there remains a demanding challenge to develop biocompatible contrast agents via a simple, green synthetic route for multi-modal imaging. Here we report the synthesis and applications of a new contrast agent for triple-modal imaging, that is, iodine-containing N-doping carbon nano-dots hybridized with Fe3O4 nanoparticles (I@CNDs-Fe3O4). We develop a one-pot, environment friendly hydrothermal method to synthesize the hybrid nanoparticles, primarily using kelp as the bioresource. I@CNDs-Fe3O4 nanoparticles have been demonstrated to exhibit excellent multi-modal imaging capabilities, including wavelength-tunable fluorescent imaging, X-ray attenuation for CT imaging enhancement, and T2-Weighted MR imaging. Importantly, the formulation of the hybrid nanoparticle provides an optimal solution to address the disequilibrium of osmotic pressure caused by the conventional CT imaging contrast agents of iodine compounds. The I@CNDs-Fe3O4 nanoparticles promise important applications in multi-modal imaging technologies in vivo as a versatile and biocompatible contrast agent.

ce:section.ce:para: Advanced in vivo imaging technologies have made revolutionary changes in both the fundamental research fields and clinical applications for disease diagnosis or prognosis [1–5]. The development of multi-modal imaging contrast agents is a rapidly growing area, promising new solutions to acquire a vision beyond the limits of human perception by combination of the merits from different medical imaging modalities [6–10]. Fluorescence (FL) [11–13], computed tomography (CT) [14], and magnetic resonance (MR) [15–18] imaging technology are among the most widely used methods in clinical practice. The fluorescence technologies provide the option of fast imaging with high spatial resolution, while being limited by the drawbacks of photo-bleaching and short tissue penetration depth [19,20]. These shortcomings could be improved by the introduction of the additional modalities of CT and MR imaging [20–22]. CT and MR images have different sensitivity in bone structures and soft tissues [20]. Both of them require scanning for image acquisition, which is typically slower than the FL imaging. It is very attractive to have the fast-switching option between these imaging modalities in order to facilitate diagnostics [23,24]. Therefore, researchers have actively been developing multi-modal imaging agents of combining fluorescence, CT and MR imaging techniques for rapid and accurate diagnosis. Yang et al. functionalized multiple core/shell structured CdS:Mn/ZnS/SiO2 fluorescent quantum dots with Gd (III) ions, which turned out to be water-soluble and low cytotoxic, but also highly fluorescent and paramagnetic for fluorescence (FL) imaging and magnetic resonance (MR) imaging [25]. Li and his coworkers developed the core/shell Fe3O4@NaLuF4: YbEr/Tm nanostructure (MUCNP) using a step-wise synthetic method. The as-prepared MUCNP could be served as an MR, CT and UCL probe for tri-modality imaging [26]. However, there remains a demanding challenge to develop multi-modal imaging contrast agents via a simple, green synthetic route for clinically translational research.

The agents containing high sequence atom, especially the iodine element, are widely used in clinical CT imaging, thanks to their excellent X-ray attenuation effect [27–29]. The iodinated contrast agents are mainly classified to two different categories: ionic and nonionic [29–31]. The ionic iodinated contrast agents suffer an inevitable shortcoming in clinical applications, due to the introduction of renal toxicity or other physiological problems by their high osmotic pressure in aqueous solutions [29,32]. The nonionic iodinated contrast agents such as Iohexol and Iopamidol have been developed to improve this critical issue, but only achieving a limited success because their osmotic pressures are still 2–3 folds higher than human plasma osmolality [32]. Therefore, it is an inviting research direction to develop a new generation of iodinated contrast agents with minimum toxicity and high cost-effectiveness by environment-friendly synthesis.

Here we report a new strategy to synthesize I@CNDs-Fe3O4 for the enhanced CT imaging performance by integrating the element of iodine into the hybrid nanoparticles through green chemistry. The homogenized kelp is introduced into the hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-containing carbon nanodots (CNDs) as the green carbonaceous precursor and nitrogen source due to its nutrition of carbohydrates and vegetable proteins. The enriched organoiodine compounds in kelp are simultaneously transformed to nonionic iodinated components in carbon nanodots [33]. The hybrid I@CNDs-Fe3O4 nanoparticles are synthesized via an environment friendly one-pot hydrothermal method, offering excellent emission-tunable fluorescent imaging, X-ray attenuation for CT imaging enhancement, and T2-Weighted MR imaging. Importantly, the formulation of the hybrid nanoparticles provides an optimal solution to address the disequilibrium of osmotic pressure caused by the conventional CT imaging contrast agents of iodine compounds. The as-prepared I@CNDs-Fe3O4 nanoparticles serve as a versatile and biocompatible imaging contrast candidate for the development of multi-modal imaging technologies.

The common sea vegetable (kelp) was purchased from a grocery store. Ammonium hydroxide solution was provided by Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O, purity >99%), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, purity >99%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. HUVEC (Human Umbilical Vein Endothelial Cells) and 4 T1 cells (a mouse mammary carcinoma cell line) were obtained from American type culture collection (ATCC). Dulbecco's modified eagle media (DMEM, High Glucose), and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were provided by Gibco. CellTiter-Glo (CTG) was purchased from Beijing promega. Paraformaldehyde (PFA) was purchased by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Regenerated cellulose dialysis membranes with molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 3,000 were acquired from Thermo Fisher Scientific. Deionized water (18 MΩ) was purified using a Milli-Q Plus 185 water purification system (Millipore, Bedford, MA). All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

I@CND-Fe3O4 was synthesized by an environment friendly hydrothermal method. Briefly, kelp (20% w/w) was smashed by the homogenizer. The kelp extraction (20 mL) was added into a solution (20 mL) containing FeCl2·4H2O (0.994 g) and FeCl3·6H2O (0.63 g). The mixture was treated with vigorous ultrasound at 60 °C for 3 h. The reaction atmosphere was protected by nitrogen to prevent undesired oxidation. Ammonium hydroxide solution (4 mol L−1) was added into the mixture for an optimal pH value (pH ≈ 8), followed by another ultrasonic treatment at 60 °C for 4 h as an aging step. The mixture was transferred into a teflon-lined autoclave for the hydrothermal reaction at 150 °C for 6 h, followed by a temperature increase to 200 °C for 18 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was filtrated through a filtering membrane (220 nm). The filtrate was dialyzed (3000 D) against deionized water for 24 h. The product of I@CND-Fe3O4 was collected by the steps of rotary evaporation and freeze-drying.
As the contrast, I@CND was synthesized through the same procedure with I@CND-Fe3O4 except the addition of FeCl2·4H2O and FeCl3·6H2O.

The SEM images were acquired by a Quant 250FEG scanning electron microscope (FEI, America). The TEM images were collected by a transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM-2100, 200 kV). Size distribution and ζ potential of the nanomaterials were measured with a Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 (Malvern, Ltd.) at room temperature. The UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired with an ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer EVOLUTION 220 (Thermo, US). Fluorescence spectra were obtained from a RF-5301PC fluoremeter (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan). The XPS data was collected using a PHI Quanter II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Ulvac-Phi, Japan) equipped with an Mg Kα excitation source. The osmotic pressures of different iodine-containing agents were determined by a standard method for comparative measurements of the freezing points of pure water and of solutions, using an osmometer (OM819, Loser Messtechnik, Germany).

HUVEC or 4T1 cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented 10% FBS, 10 U/mL penicillin, and 10 mg mL−1 streptomycin. The cell cultures were maintained in 37 °C incubator with 95% humidity and 5% CO2. The cells (7–10 × 103cells per well) were seeded in a 96-well plate, followed by an incubation of 24 h for cell adhesion. Aliquots of the DMEM medium (10% FBS, 1% antibiotics) containing different concentration of I@CND-Fe3O4 (0, 100, 200, 400, 600, 800 μg mL−1) were applied as replacement for the cell cultures. After the incubation as specified elsewhere (24 h or 48 h), CTG assay was performed on the cells using a microplate reader (Varioskan Flash, Thermo Fisher, America) by referring to a standard protocol provided by the supplier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The in vitro and in vivo fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro fluorescence imaging was performed on HUVEC and 4 T1 cells. The cells were seeded on the confocal petri-dishes, and treated with the nanomaterials in a procedure similar to the description as above. The cells were washed with PBS buffer for three times, and fixed with PFA solution (4% w/w) for about 15 min. After washing twice with PBS again, the slides were mounted onto the stage of a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) (Leica TCS SP8) for fluorescent image acquisition. Different excitation wavelengths, including 405, 488, 522 nm, were tested on the treated cell samples.

in vivo fluorescent imaging was demonstrated on the zebra fish embryos in the hatching period of 48 h post-fertilization (hpf), which were treated with I@CNDs-Fe3O4 (1 mL, 100 μg mL−1) for 12 h. The fluorescent images of the embryos were acquired using a confocal laser scanning microscope (Leica TCS SP8). Different sets of excitation/emission channels (excitation wavelengths including 405, 488, 522 nm; emission wavelength ranges including 420–500, 500–600, 622–760 nm) were tested on the zebra fish embryos. After the embryos hatched to larva (typically 72hpf), they were transported into the dishes in fresh water without I@CNDs-Fe3O4. Their fluorescent images were acquired at a series time points such as 0, 0.5, 2, 6, 18, 36 h.

The CT signals of I@CND-Fe3O4 suspended in agarose gel (2% w/w) were collected on an X-ray micro-CT scanner (Skyscan 1176, Bruker, Germany). The imaging parameters were set by referring to the instrumental manual datasheet. I@CNDs-Fe3O4 and Iohexol (Normalized Iodine: 0, 0.225, 0.45, 0.9, 1.8, 3.6 mg mL−1) in the gel were placed in the 6-wells cuvette for the in vitro assessment. Hounsfield unit (HU) values were recorded for quantitative analysis the contrast enhancement.

The in vivo CT imaging was performed on the adult zebra fish (4 months) which was pre-treated with I@CNDs-Fe3O4 for 2 h. The fish without pre-treatment were tested as the blank control. The CT images in the longitudinal or transverse planes were selected by referring to the fish structural characteristics, with a schematic drawing in the appropriate section.

The in vitro MRI data of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 in gel was assessed by using a 7.0 T MRI scanner (Bruker Biospin, Germany). The agarose gel samples containing I@CNDs-Fe3O4 were prepared according to the description as above. T2-weighted images were acquired by adopting the rapid imaging with refocused echoes (RARE) sequence with the parameters as follows: repetition time (TR) of 3000.0 ms, echo time (TE) of 18.0 ms, flip angle (FA) of 90°, section thickness of 1.0 mm, field of view (FOV) of 4.85 cm and matrix (MTX) of 128 × 128 pixels. The r2 was determined by the curve fitting of 1/T2 versus the iron concentration.

The in vivo MRI was performed on the adult zebra fishes (4 months) with or without the pre-incubation of I@CNDs-Fe3O4. The relaxivities of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 and in the MR images of zebra fishes were collected by referring to a home-optimized protocol with parameters: repetition time (TR) of 3000.0 ms, echo time (TE) of 36.0 ms, flip angle (FA) of 90°, section thickness of 1.0 mm, field of view (FOV) of 3.32 cm and matrix (MTX) of 256 × 256 pixels. The MR images in the longitudinal or transverse planes of fish were determined similarly to the description as above.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, I@CNDs-Fe3O4 can be synthesized by an environment friendly hydrothermal method. Homogenized kelp is selected as the primary raw material, providing carbon, nitrogen, and iodine sources for synthesis of the hybrid nanoparticles. The reaction only requires simple and common chemicals, including FeCl3, FeCl2, and ammonia. The synthesis is performed in the temperature range between 150 °C and 200 °C, a relatively mild hydrothermal condition. All the other experimental steps only include ultrasonication, filtration, and dialysis. Through this one-pot, green synthetic route, the product is composed of nitrogen-containing carbon nanodots which also integrate nonionic iodinated compounds and Fe3O4, thus being abbreviated as I@CNDs-Fe3O4 hybrid nanoparticles. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image indicates a subrotund morphology for I@CNDs-Fe3O4. The crystalline lattice fringe with lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is identified, corresponding to the (100) facet of the graphite carbon [34–38]. In addition, the crystalline lattice fringe with lattice spacing of 0.47 nm is identified, corresponding to the (111) lattice plane of Fe3O4 [39]. I@CNDs-Fe3O4 nanoparticles exhibit an overall uniform size distribution in the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1c). The corresponding histograms of the particle size distribution of the TEM image is shown in Fig. 1d. It was found that size distribution of nanoparticles was fairly narrow, and the average size was calculated to be 7.21 nm. “The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 and I@CNDs is shown in Fig. 1e The XRD pattern of I@CNDs shows only a broaden peak in 25.22° assigned to amorphous CNDs. And there are additional diffraction peaks at around 30.18°, 35.29°, 43.18°, 56.92°, and 62.91° in the powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of I@CNDs-Fe3O4, assigned to the (220), (311), (400), (511), and (440) crystal planes of Fe3O4, respectively.” The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image (Fig. 1f) provides a further zoomed-out view of the size and morphology of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The hydrodynamic size of as-prepared I@CNDs-Fe3O4 is approximately 20 nm in deionized water by dynamic light scattering (DLS). It is slightly larger than the size measurement by electron microscopy, due to the hydration effect in different sample preparation methods. There is only a minimal size change (nearly 38 nm after 30 days in the shelf) for the I@CNDs-Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The poly-dispersity index was measured either at the 1st day (PDI: 0.195) or at the 30th day (PDI: 0.213), indicating an excellent dispersion and stability of the product in the aqueous suspension. The zeta potentials (ζ) of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 in deionized water were determined to be −43.2 (at the 1st day) and − 41.6 mV (at the 30th day), respectively. The negative charges on the nanoparticle surface may assist maintaining a good dispersion capability of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 by electrostatic repulsion.

The UV spectra of both I@CNDs-Fe3O4 and I@CNDs exhibit two peaks at 320 nm and 372 nm, respectively (Fig. 2 a). The result indicates that coupling Fe3O4 would not compromise the inherent band gaps inside I@CNDs. The peaks at 320 and 372 nm can be attributed to the n–π* electron transition of CO and CN, which serve as the energy traps [40]. The trapping of excited state energy by the surface states results in strong FL property of the I@CNDs-Fe3O4. As shown in Fig. 2b, the fluorescent spectra of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 exhibit an interesting behavior of both emission wavelength (400 nm–600 nm) and intensity tunable by the excitation wavelength. This phenomenon is consistent with the previous reports in the literature [34,41]. In our tests using different excitation wavelengths, the most intensive emission of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 was collected at 440 nm approximately by excitation at 380 nm. Several previous reports suggested that the N-doping and some chemical moieties such as –OH and –COOH on the surface of CNDs may serve as energy traps, endowing the composite nanoparticles with strong fluorescence in a defect-induced emission mechanism [42–44]. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 was determined to be 22.3% by a standard method for absolute QY, as convincing evidence for the super fluorescent property. The time-resolved photoluminescence measurements were taken to investigate the fluorescent lifetime of the I@CNDs-Fe3O4, suggesting short lifetime values (τ1 = 1.05 ns and τ2 = 4.53 ns, in Fig. 2c). The results indicated the radiative recombination nature of excitations in the fluorescent process. The wavelength-tunable FL property with broad excitation wavelength choices may find good applications in multiplexed bio-imaging.

We further evaluated the photo-stability of the as-prepared I@CNDs-Fe3O4. As shown in Fig. 2d, the fluorescence intensity of fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC, an organic dye as the control) rapidly dropped to only 40% of the original value in 4 min, and further decreased to the undetectable level after 20 min under UV irradiation (250 W). In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of the I@CNDs-Fe3O4 still maintained approximately 55% of the original intensity even after UV irradiation for 2 h. There was a slight fluorescent rise in the first 4 min of UV irradiation, which might be attributed to the defect changes on the surface of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The confocal images of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 by UV exposure (Fig. 2e) consistently demonstrated the superior photostability of the I@CNDs-Fe3O4 after irradiation of 24 h, promising important applications which request long-term and real-time bioimaging.

The superparamagnetic property of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 was determined by a standard test for magnetization under the external magnetic fields, which featured the absence of a hysteresis loop. As shown in Fig. 2f, the magnetization M (H) curve exhibits a relatively sharp relaxation, with a very high value of saturation magnetization (Ms, nearly 67 emu g−1 at room temperature). Therefore, the as-prepared I@CNDs-Fe3O4 promises important applications as an excellent MRI contrast agent.

The chemical compositions of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 were investigated by X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the full-range XPS spectra of both I@CNDs-Fe3O4 and I@CNDs exhibit the characteristic peaks corresponding to the binding energies of C1s, N1s, O1s, and I3d5. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s presents several peaks identified at 283.5, 285.1, 287.2 eV for the binding energy, corresponding to the CC/CC, CN, OCO, respectively (Fig. 3b) [45]. The result indicates the presence of multiple chemical groups, especially the successful incorporation of nitrogen-containing groups in the carbon nanodots. In Fig. 3c, the high-resolution spectrum of N1s can be deconvolved to two peaks (399 eV attributed to CNC and 401 eV attributed to CN group). Semi-quantitative analysis of the C1s and N1s peaks suggests that the atomic ratio of C/N is approximately 8:1, supporting the nitrogen-rich composition for the as-prepared product. Successful N-doping in the carbon nanodots is beneficial for the fluorescent property because the nitrogen-containing chemical groups may boost defect-induced emission mechanism.

The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s (Fig. 3d) is contributed by the peaks at 530.9 eV and 532.9 eV, which corresponds to CO and CO, respectively [44]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of I3d is identified to include two peaks at 628.9 eV and 631.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 3e). The peak split corresponds to the orbital spilt iodine 3d to 3d3/2 and3d5/2 levels, corresponding to the covalently bound iodine [46]. The distinct Fe2p peaks in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 include 724.8 and 710.9 eV (Fig. 3f), which are the characteristic doublet of Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 for iron oxide. The detailed analyses of the high-resolution XPS spectra provide solid evidence for successful coupling of I and Fe in the final product, which is crucial for the enhanced multi-modal imaging capability of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 as the contrast agent.

Huang et al. studied the effects of osmotic pressure produced by different contrast agents on the deformability of erythrocyte. The results showed that high osmotic pressure contrast media decrease the deformability of red blood cells, which leads to many disorders such as thrombosis and ischemic heart disease [47–49]. Therefore, osmotic pressure is an important parameter for the administration of imaging contrast agents in clinical practice. Two representative iodinate contrast agents in clinics, including KI (the first generation, ionic) and Iohexol (the second generation, nonionic), were selected as the controls for comparison with I@CNDs-Fe3O4. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the osmotic pressure of the KI increases from 400 mOsm to nearly 3000 mOsm in the concentration range of 25–200 mg mL−1. In the higher concentrations of KI (>200 mg mL−1), the osmotic pressure values exceed the work range of the osmometer. A zoomed-in view of the osmotic pressures of Iohexol and I@CNDs-Fe3O4 is available in Fig. 4b. As nonionic imaging contrast agents, both of them exhibit much lower osmotic pressures than KI. However, given the most widely used two concentrations (300 mg mL−1, or 350 mg mL−1 normalized for iodine) in clinical practice, the osmotic pressures of Iohexol are determined to be nearly 600 mOsm, or 800 mOsm, respectively. Either of them is still two folds higher than normal human plasma osmolality (275–299 mOsm) [50]. In contrast, the osmotic pressure of I@CND-Fe3O4 is much closer to the human plasma osmolality in these clinically relevant concentrations. Therefore, it offers better clinical utilization by reducing the undesired side effects of osmotic dis-equilibrium, in comparison with both the first and second generations of iodinated contrast agents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cytotoxicity and fluorescent performance of I@CND-Fe3O4
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 was examined on Human Umbilical Vein Endothelial Cells (HUVEC) and 4T1 mammary carcinoma cells by using the standard Cell Tilter-Glo (CTG) luminescent cell viability assay. Cell cultures were treated with different concentrations of I@CND-Fe3O4 (0, 100, 200, 400, 600, 800 μg mL−1) in the 96-well plates. As shown in Fig. 5 a-b, both of these two cell lines maintain nearly 100% cell viability for all of the concentration series after 1 d incubation. Their changes in cellular viability are almost negligible after 2 d incubation as well. The experiments suggest a very low cytotoxicity of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 for different cell lines. The fluorescence imaging performance of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 was demonstrated on HUVEC and 4 T1 cells by CLSM (Fig. 5c-d). HUVEC and 4 T1 cells were incubated with 100 μg mL−1 I@CNDs-Fe3O4 (suspended in DMEM high glucose medium) for 24 h. The fluorescent images suggest that I@CNDs-Fe3O4 can be internalized by the cells, with a predominant distribution in cytoplasm. Both the HUVEC and 4 T1 cells maintain good cell morphology after internalization of I@CND-Fe3O4, without obvious apoptosis related changes. These observations support that I@CNDs-Fe3O4 nanoparticles possess an excellent biocompatibility in the cell experiments [51,52]. As shown in Fig. 5c-d, distinct and clean fluorescent staining of the cells at different channels (blue, green, and red) can be harvested in an excitation wavelength-tunable manner (at 405 nm, 488 nm, and 522 nm, respectively). The results further validate the characteristic wavelength-tunable fluorescence property of I@CNDs-Fe3O4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The in vivo fluorescence imaging of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 on the zebra fish model
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent imaging performance of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 in vivo was demonstrated on zebra fish, an important vertebrate model in biology [38,53–55]. At 48 hpf, the embryos of zebra fish were incubated with I@CNDs-Fe3O4 (100 μg mL−1). This time point was defined as −24 h (before the termination of the nanoparticles). After incubated with I@CNDs-Fe3O4 for 12 h, the unfertilized zebra fish embryos were collected for CLSM images. As shown in Fig. 6 a, I@CNDs-Fe3O4 nanoparticles were distributed on the embryo membrane, but also inside the embryo. After another 12 h, the embryos were successfully hatched to zebra larvaes without detectable morphological dis-shape in the bodies, suggesting good biocompatibility of I@CND-Fe3O4 in vivo. Then the zebra larvaes were sampled for CLSM images at a series of time points. As shown in Fig. 6b-g, the larvaes were fluorescently stained by I@CNDs-Fe3O4, mainly in the gills and esophagi. The wavelength-tunable fluorescence property of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 was evident in zebra embryo and larvaes, excited by the laser at different wavelength channels. The experiments suggested an interesting pattern for the uptake and translocation of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 nanoparticles in zebra larvaes. After stoping administration of nanoparticles with fresh water, the initially accumulated I@CNDs-Fe3O4 in the gills faded away in 0.5 h. It indicated that the adsorption of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 on the gill structures might not be permanent, thus allowing nanoparticles to be exchanged into the environment. The fluorescent images also provided vivid views of the concentration changes I@CNDs-Fe3O4 along the esophagi at those time points, leading to the assumption that I@CNDs-Fe3O4 could be removed from the larva bodies via the digestive system.

The performance of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 nanoparticles in CT imaging in vitro and in vivo was investigated, using a high-resolution X-ray micro-CT scanner (Fig. 7 ). Fig. 7a presents a schematic drawing of the longitudinal planes (LP) or transverse planes (TP) of a zebra fish as the references for CT images. The EP tubes loaded with different concentrations of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 in agarose gel prostheses were imaged for in vitro CT evaluation. As shown in Fig. 7b, the performance of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 in CT contrast imaging exhibited a concentration-dependent trend (Normalized Iodine: 0, 0.225, 0.45, 0.9, 1.8, 3.6 mg mL−1). In Fig. 7c, the brightness of the CT images for I@CNDs-Fe3O4 and Iohexol was quantified for comparison. The slope of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 was calculated to be 180 HULg−1 approximately, higher than that of Iohexol (162 HULg−1). This value is nearly seven folds higher than the property from a previous report (26.1 HULg−1, C-Fe3O4 QDs hybrid nanoparticles) in the literature [41], promising significantly improved sensitivity in the CT image contrast enhancement due to iodine doping. For the CT imaging experiments in vivo, zebra fish were fed with the fish food with or without I@CND-Fe3O4 nanoparticles. As highlighted by the red dashed circles (Fig. 7d), there were intensive CT signals in both transversal and longitudinal planes of the fish fed with I@CND-Fe3O4 nanoparticles. In contrast, no detectable CT signal enhancement was observed in the identical body parts of the untreated fish. The signals concentrated on the digestive tract of zebra fish. All observations above can further confirm that the obtained I@CNDs-Fe3O4 can serve as promising candidates for CT imaging.

The performance of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 in MR imaging was tested by a 7.0 T MRI scanner (with a T2-weighted mode). I@CNDs-Fe3O4 nanoparticles suspended in the agarose gel prostheses were imaged in gradient concentrations (0, 25, 50, 100, 200, and 400 μM, normalized by Fe element with ICP-MS). As shown in Fig. 8 a, the T2-weighted MR images evidently feature a trend of enhanced darkness dependent on the concentrations of nanoparticles (normalized to Fe elemental contents). Quantitative analysis of the inverse relaxation times (1/T2) in those MR images suggests a linear relationship with the normalized concentrations of Fe (Fig. 8b). The transverse relaxivities (r2, the slope in the linear fitting in Fig. 8b) is calculated to be 177.4 mM−1 s−1, comparable to the reported values in the successful examples of MRI contrast agents [41,56]. The application of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 in MR imaging in vivo was demonstrated on the same groups of zebra fish described in the section of CT imaging, including the positive sample group and negative control group [57]. In Fig. 8c, there are areas of interest on the abdomen of the fish (highlighted by the red circles) with a remarkable darkening effect for the fish treated with I@CNDs-Fe3O4. In contrast, there is no such detectable MRI signal in the identical body parts for the untreated fish. MR images on both the TP and LP provide the cross references for the validation of the detected signals in these fish groups. The MRI results suggest that the uptake and translocation behaviors of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 are consistent with the previous observations by in vivo fluorescence imaging and CT imaging. Therefore, the as-prepared I@CND-Fe3O4 nanoparticles allow for unique multi-modal imaging, featuring wavelength-tunable fluorescence, non-ionic iodinated compound enhanced CT, and effective T2-weighted MRI signals simultaneously.

The well-designed combination of different imaging modalities may help overcome their individual limitations, and gain extra benefits for disease diagnosis and medical practice [6,20,58]. The conventional in vivo fluorescent imaging technologies possess the advantages of high spatial resolution (2–3 mm) and rapid imaging (s-min), but suffer from the limitations such as photo-bleaching and short tissue penetration depth (<2 cm). These shortcomings can be improved by the functionalization of the FL contrast agents with the additional modalities of CT and MR imaging. CT imaging is relatively fast (seconds to minutes), allowing deep tissue penetration (unlimited), but limited by the administration of X-ray radiation facilites [59–61]. MR imaging is also able to visualize the target tissues deeply inside the body with decent spatial resolution (25–100 μm), but requires long time for image acquisition and processing (minutes to hours) [20,62]. CT and MR images are supplementary to each other in differentiating bone structures and soft tissues. Therefore, the fast-switching option between these imaging modalities would facilitate diagnostic clarification in the challenging examples such as complex joint fractures or tumor metastasis. It also helps reduce the burden of loading imaging contrast agents separately into the patients' bodies and save the precious time for diagnosis and treatments. Importantly, the contrast agents of CT/MRI technologies mutually welcome the incorporation of the FL imaging modality, especially in the emerging field of image-guided surgery. In these clinical bedside scenarios, the tissue penetration depth is no longer a limiting factor for FL imaging because even deep tissues are exposed in the surgery. The strengths of fast, high-resolution imaging by fluorescence could offer the surgeons with real-time, clear visualization of the tumor sites from the normal tissues. The as-prepared I@CND-Fe3O4 nanoparticles provide a clever strategy of adopting the merits of the FL/ CT / MR triple-modal imaging techniques collectively, thus being of great significance in biomedical diagnosis.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized a new biocompatible material of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 as contrast agents for multi-modal imaging. The synthesis of I@CNDs-Fe3O4 nanoparticles features a one-pot, environment friendly hydrothermal method using kelp as the primary bioresource. High performance of the hybrid nanoparticles has been demonstrated in vitro and in vivo for wavelength-tunable fluorescent imaging, X-ray attenuation for CT imaging enhancement, and T2-Weighted MR imaging. Importantly, I@CNDs-Fe3O4 nanoparticles solve a critical limitation of conventional CT imaging contrast agents of iodine compounds by providing an optimal solution to maintain the osmotic pressure physiologically. As a versatile and biocompatible imaging contrast candidate, the hybrid nanoparticles offer new opportunities for the development of multi-modal imaging technologies.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51873087 and 51572124), Natural Science Foundation of China Jiangsu Province (grant number BE2018051), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (grant number 30920130121001), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (grant number 2018M632307), and a Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD, China).
